content.text: Chat-format language models receive their input not as raw text but as a structured conversation with named participants. Every message carries a
role
system
user
, or
assistant

— that tells the model who said what and what weight to give it. The model never sees JSON: the chat template serialises this structured conversation into a single flat token sequence, injecting special delimiter tokens between roles. Understanding the three roles and how they map to the raw token stream is the foundation for writing effective prompts and debugging unexpected model behaviour.
The Three Roles
Role
Who it represents
Typical content
Trust level
system
The application developer / operator
Persona, constraints, background context, tool instructions
Highest — trained to follow system instructions even when user asks to ignore them
user
The end user of the application
Questions, requests, uploaded content, tool results in some APIs
Medium — followed unless it conflicts with system or safety constraints
assistant
The model's prior turns in a multi-turn conversation
Previous model responses; can also be used for prefill to steer next response
Model continues from where assistant turn left off
API-Level Role Syntax

import anthropic
from openai import OpenAI

# ── Anthropic ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# system is a top-level parameter; messages alternate user/assistant
client_anthropic = anthropic.Anthropic()

response = client_anthropic.messages.create(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    max_tokens=512,
    system="You are a Python tutor. Keep answers concise. Use code examples.",
    messages=[
        {"role": "user",      "content": "What is a list comprehension?"},
        {"role": "assistant", "content": "A list comprehension builds a list in one line:"},
        {"role": "user",      "content": "Show me an example with filtering."},
    ],
)
print(response.content[0].text)

# ── OpenAI ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# system is a message like any other, with role='system'
client_openai = OpenAI()

response = client_openai.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=[
        {"role": "system",    "content": "You are a Python tutor. Keep answers concise."},
        {"role": "user",      "content": "What is a list comprehension?"},
        {"role": "assistant", "content": "A list comprehension builds a list in one line:"},
        {"role": "user",      "content": "Show me an example with filtering."},
    ],
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)
How Chat Templates Serialise Roles to Tokens

The model never receives a Python dict. The chat template converts the structured message list into a flat string with role-specific delimiters, then tokenises it. Each model family uses its own format. The HuggingFace
apply_chat_template
method reads the template from the tokeniser config and handles this serialisation:

from transformers import AutoTokenizer

messages = [
    {"role": "system",    "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
    {"role": "user",      "content": "What is 2+2?"},
    {"role": "assistant", "content": "4."},
    {"role": "user",      "content": "What is 3+3?"},
]

# ── Mistral 7B format ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
mistral_tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2")
print("Mistral:")
print(repr(mistral_tok.apply_chat_template(messages, tokenize=False)))
# <s>[INST] You are a helpful assistant.
#
# What is 2+2? [/INST] 4.</s>[INST] What is 3+3? [/INST]
# Note: Mistral v0.1 folds system into the first [INST] block

# ── LLaMA 3 format ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
llama_tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct")
print("\nLLaMA 3:")
print(repr(llama_tok.apply_chat_template(messages, tokenize=False)))
# <|begin_of_text|><|start_header_id|>system<|end_header_id|>
# You are a helpful assistant.<|eot_id|>
# <|start_header_id|>user<|end_header_id|>
# What is 2+2?<|eot_id|>
# <|start_header_id|>assistant<|end_header_id|>
# 4.<|eot_id|>
# <|start_header_id|>user<|end_header_id|>
# What is 3+3?<|eot_id|>
# <|start_header_id|>assistant<|end_header_id|>   ← model generates from here

# add_generation_prompt=True appends the opening assistant header
# so the model knows to continue in the assistant role
prompt_with_gen = llama_tok.apply_chat_template(
    messages,
    tokenize=False,
    add_generation_prompt=True,
)
The ChatML Format
ChatML (introduced by OpenAI) became a de facto standard for open-source fine-tunes. It uses
<|im_start|>
and
<|im_end|>
as delimiters ("im" = "imaginary monologue"). Many models — Qwen, Phi-3, Yi — use this format:

<|im_start|>system
You are a helpful assistant.<|im_end|>
<|im_start|>user
What is 2+2?<|im_end|>
<|im_start|>assistant
4.<|im_end|>
<|im_start|>user
What is 3+3?<|im_end|>
<|im_start|>assistant
Assistant Prefill: Steering the Response

Because the model generates tokens that continue the assistant role, you can supply the beginning of the assistant turn (a "prefill") to constrain the response format. The model is then forced to continue from that starting point:

import anthropic
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Force JSON output by prefilling the assistant turn with "{"
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    max_tokens=256,
    system="Return only valid JSON. No explanation.",
    messages=[
        {
            "role": "user",
            "content": "Give me the capitals of France, Germany, and Italy.",
        },
        {
            "role": "assistant",
            "content": "{",   # ← prefill: model must continue from here
        },
    ],
)
# The response starts from where the prefill left off
raw = "{" + response.content[0].text
data = json.loads(raw)
print(data)
# {"France": "Paris", "Germany": "Berlin", "Italy": "Rome"}

# Prefill also steers tone and format:
# assistant prefill "Sure! Here's a step-by-step breakdown:\n1."
# forces the model into a numbered list format.

The three roles encode a trust hierarchy: system sets the rules, user makes requests within those rules, and assistant continues the conversation. This separation is what allows the same model to serve as a children's tutor, a code reviewer, and a customer support agent — only the system prompt changes. Misunderstanding the hierarchy is a common prompt-engineering mistake: instructions placed in the user role carry less weight than the same instructions in the system role, and assistant prefill can be used to override response format without touching the system prompt at all.
